event: radiograph of his pelvis revealed an undisplaced fracture of his left acetabulum of both the anterior and posterior columns
warmed Hartmanns one litre infusion commenced
initial delay due to pressure in the Emergency department
referred to the Orthopaedic on call team
blood pressure dropped to 75/51 mm Hg
haemorrhagic shock had not been considered
treated for sepsis in the Emergency department
arterial blood pressure was 90/60 mm Hg
left lower abdominal quadrant swollen with ecchymosis
a diagnosis of haemorrhagic shock was made
2 adult therapeutic doses of platelets transfused
noradrenaline infusion to maintain MAP > 65 mm Hg
A CT scan was deemed necessary for diagnosis and management planning
CT abdomen confirmed the fracture of the left acetabulum
significant haematoma on left pelvic sidewall adjacent to the fracture
8 mm enhancing nodule suggestive of an internal iliac artery pseudoaneurysm
ill-defined blush of contrast within the haematoma
extra-peritoneal haematoma inseparable from the bladder
transferred to the intensive care unit
CT Angiogram demonstrated no arterial bleed
gelfoam embolization of anterior and posterior division segmental branches of the left internal iliac artery
consultation with the regional pelvic surgeon
uncontrollable bleeding from his pin site
continuous pressure applied to the wound
death due to chronic liver disease
